title: Knee Arthroscopy Diagnostic Sequence and Portals
summary: Systematic diagnostic arthroscopy of the knee: patellofemoral, medial, lateral, notch compartments. Key for meniscectomy, ACL, etc.
must_know: Standard portals: anterolateral (AL) viewing, anteromedial (AM) working; accessory for posterior or meniscus.

Diagnostic sequence: suprapatellar pouch, patellofemoral joint, medial gutter, medial compartment (MFC, MM, MTP), intercondylar notch (ACL/PCL), lateral compartment (LFC, LM, LTP), posteromedial/lateral if needed.
Structures at risk: saphenous nerve/vein (medial portals), peroneal nerve/lateral inferior genicular (lateral), popliteal structures (posterior).
Meniscus pathology: bucket handle, radial, horizontal, root tears - must probe and visualize fully before resection/repair.

structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Medial portal placement; neuroma or numbness risk.
Posterior compartment work or stray instruments.

landmarks.landmark: Patella, tibial tubercle, joint line, fibular head

safe_zones_or_intervals: AL portal: soft spot lateral to patellar tendon, 1cm above joint line.
AM portal: under arthroscopic visualization to avoid MFC and MM damage.
danger_zones: Transverse medial incision too posterior risks saphenous; lateral too distal risks peroneal.
procedure_specific_relevance: For meniscectomy: identify tear type, stability, and relation to popliteus or MCL before partial resection.
For ACL: assess notch, ACL footprint, associated meniscus/chondral injury.
common_pimp_questions: Describe the systematic diagnostic arthroscopy sequence of the knee.
What structures are at risk with anteromedial portal placement?
retrieval_phrases: knee arthroscopy portals diagnostic sequence meniscectomy
knee arthroscopy structures at risk saphenous peroneal
notes: Synthesized from existing v2_1 ACL/meniscus/rotator entries and standard OB sports knee knowledge in source files. Full portal anatomy and images would benefit from dedicated OB arthroscopy pages.

title: Shoulder Arthroscopy Diagnostic Sequence and Portals (for Rotator Cuff)
summary: Posterior viewing portal primary; systematic glenohumeral and subacromial diagnostic before cuff repair.
must_know: Posterior portal: 2cm inferior/medial to posterolateral acromion corner (soft spot).
Anterior portal: under visualization in rotator interval or subscapularis region.
Lateral (subacromial) portal for bursa and cuff work.
Diagnostic: biceps, labrum, rotator cuff undersurface, glenoid, humeral head, subacromial space (bursa, cuff bursal side, AC joint).

structures_at_risk.why_it_matters: Inferior portals or excessive inferior retraction; deltoid function critical post-op for cuff.
Large/massive tears or releases at notch.

landmarks: Acromion, scapular spine, coracoid, AC joint, greater tuberosity.
safe_zones_or_intervals: Posterior portal safe in soft spot; stay lateral to avoid suprascapular.
danger_zones: Inferior glenohumeral work risks axillary; medial subacromial risks suprascapular.
procedure_specific_relevance: For rotator cuff: assess tear size, shape (U, L, crescent), retraction, tissue quality, and associated biceps/labral pathology before repair decision (debride, repair, or reconstruct).
common_pimp_questions: What is the primary viewing portal for shoulder arthroscopy and why?
Name three structures at risk during portal placement or subacromial decompression.
retrieval_phrases: shoulder arthroscopy portals diagnostic sequence rotator cuff
missing_needed_sources: Dedicated OB shoulder arthroscopy portal anatomy and systematic diagnostic checklist pages (current v2_1 rotator data is more open/mini-open focused).
notes: Based on existing rotator_cuff_repair entry in v2_1 and general shoulder anatomy from source. Thin on exact portal distances and images.
